title: Dyeing with sloe berries
subtitle: A dusky grey-blue to soft purple from the berries of blackthorn on alum-mordanted wool
excerpt: Sloe berries (Prunus spinosa) yield a soft grey-blue to dusty purple on alum-mordanted wool. The colour is relatively fugitive compared to tannin-based dyes but produces a beautiful muted tone well suited to decorative textiles. Harvest after the first frost when the berries are fully ripe.
sourceNotes: Sloe berry dyeing described in Jenny Dean, Wild Colour (1999, in-copyright, substance only referenced), and in general British hedgerow dyeing literature. The anthocyanin content of sloe berries is the dye source; the grey-blue colour shift on alum is documented in multiple natural dye guides.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt that forms a bond between the wool fibre and the dye. Alum is the standard mordant for anthocyanin-based dyes including sloes.

The group of plant pigments responsible for red, purple, and blue colours in many berries and flowers. Anthocyanins are pH-sensitive: they shift from pink in acid conditions to blue-grey in neutral or slightly alkaline conditions.
The pot of water containing the extracted dye material. For sloe berries, the dye bath is a deep purple liquid; the colour on the fibre is much more muted than the bath colour.

content.text: wool a soft grey-blue or dusty mauve. The colour will be quieter than the intensely dark

suggests; anthocyanin dyes are always more muted on fibre than in solution. The tone is lovely for heathery, natural-palette textiles. Harvest sloes after the first frost, when the skin has softened slightly; pre-frost sloes are astringent and give less dye.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Use dedicated dye-pots. Wear gloves and apron. Alum is low-hazard; keep out of reach of food and children.

content.text: Dissolve 15 g alum in hot water and add to the dye-pot with enough cold water to cover the fibre loosely. Add the pre-wetted wool and raise the temperature to 80 degrees Celsius over 30 minutes. Hold for 45 minutes. Allow to cool before removing the fibre.

Place the sloe berries in a clean dye-pot and crush them lightly with a potato masher. Cover with cold water and bring slowly to 70 degrees Celsius. Hold for 30 minutes, then strain out the berry pulp, pressing the residue through a sieve to extract as much juice as possible.

Add the mordanted, still-damp fibre to the strained dye bath. Keep the temperature below 70 degrees Celsius throughout dyeing; higher temperatures shift anthocyanin dyes toward brown. Hold for 30 to 45 minutes, stirring gently every 10 minutes. Remove the fibre and rinse in water of the same temperature, then progressively cooler water. Dry in shade.
The alum bath and the sloe berry dye bath can be composted or disposed of via the foul drain in small home quantities. Both are low-hazard plant and mineral materials at domestic scale.

The colour of anthocyanin dyes shifts with pH. A teaspoon of cream of tartar in the dye bath acidifies the solution and shifts the colour slightly toward pink-mauve. A teaspoon of washing soda alkalifies it and shifts toward more grey-blue. The shift is subtle but consistent.

items.problem: The colour is brown rather than grey-blue or mauve
items.cause: The dye bath temperature exceeded 70 degrees Celsius, which degrades anthocyanins
items.fix: Use a thermometer and keep the bath below 70 degrees Celsius throughout. Anthocyanins break down at higher temperatures.

items.cause: Anthocyanin dyes are moderately fugitive; insufficient mordanting
items.fix: Ensure the alum mordant bath was held at the correct temperature for the full 45 minutes. An alum-tannin mordant combination gives better fastness with berry dyes.
